content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
October 4,
1994
Linda Gonzales,
City Clerk
City of Isleton
P.O.
Box 716
Isleton,
CA
95641
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A-94-298
Dear Ms.
Gonzales:
This is in response to your request for advice on behalf of
Isleton Mayor Leonard Maxey under the conflict—of—interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act.
1
!
You stated in a
telephone conversation on September 15,
1994 that you were
authorized to seek this advice on behalf of Mayor Maxey.
OUESTION
Does a conflict of interest exist for Mayor Maxey if his
spouse is employed by the city’s Precision Inspection department?
CONCLUSION
The salary the mayor’s spouse receives from the city is not
income under the Act and generally does not create a conflict of
interest.
The mayor may not, however, participate in decisions to
hire, fire, promote,
demote,
or discipline his spouse,
or to set a
salary for the spouse which is different from salaries paid to
other employees of the city in the same job classification or
position.
1/
Government Code Sections 81000—91015.
All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections 18000—18954.
All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804.0807 •
(916) 322-5660


File No.
A—94—298
Page
2
DISCUSSION
Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making,
participating in, or using their official position to influence a
governmental decision in which they know or have reason to know
they have a financial interest.
An official has a financial interest in a governmental
decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally,
on the official,
or on a member of the official’s immediate
family, or on:
(c)
Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars
($250)
or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
The definition of “income” under the Act provides that
the income of an individual also includes any community
property interest in the income of a spouse.
Thus,
any
person or business who has been a source of income to the
mayor’s spouse of $500 or more within the past 12 months is a
potentially disqualifying economic interest as described in
Section 87103(c).
However,
the Act expressly exempts salary from a state,
local or federal government agency from the definition of
“income.”
(Section 82030(b)(2).)
Thus, the spouse’s salary
from the city would not create a conflict of interest.
However, the mayor may not participate in decisions to hire,
fire, promote,
demote,
or discipline his or her spouse,
or to
set a salary for the spouse which is different from salaries
paid to other employees of the city in the same job
classification or position.
(Regulation 18702.1(c) (1).)
Please note, this advice is limited to the provisions of
the Act.
Other legal provisions,
such as Section 1090,
may
restrict the mayor’s ability to participate in the decision.
The Commission,
however,
does not administer this section.


File No.
A—94—298
Page
3
The district attorney and the Attorney General are
responsible for enforcing Section 1090.
I trust this letter has provided you with the guidance
you requested.
If you have further questions regarding this
matter, please contact me at
(916)
322-5901.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
Jeanette E. Turvill
Political Reform Consultant
Legal Division
JET/jt

spouse is employed by the city’s Precision Inspection department?

CONCLUSION: The salary the mayor’s spouse receives from the city is not
income under the Act and generally does not create a conflict of
interest.
The mayor may not, however, participate in decisions to
hire, fire, promote,
demote,
or discipline his spouse,
or to set a
salary for the spouse which is different from salaries paid to
other employees of the city in the same job classification or
